### Glossary

- **Geometric Brownian Motion (GBM).** Prices move by random percentage
  shocks. Two annualised parameters: drift $\mu$ (expected growth rate)
  and volatility $\sigma$ (size of the jitter). This is the price model
  underneath Black–Scholes.
- **Monte Carlo simulation.** Generate many independent random
  trajectories of $S_t$, compute each book's P&L on each, and read
  statistics off the sample. Sample error shrinks as
  $1/\sqrt{n_{\mathrm{paths}}}$.
- **$I_T := \int_0^T S_t\,dt$.** The area under the price curve over
  the horizon. It is the single random kernel that drives every
  stochastic book on this site: the fee book is linear in $I_T$, the
  back-to-back principal book is affine in $I_T$, and the
  partially-hedged book is an affine mix of the two.
- **Coverage $\tau_{\mathrm{cov}} := \min(1,\, k / (N \cdot P))$.** Fraction
  of the horizon that the pre-purchased inventory lasts under deterministic
  demand. $\tau_{\mathrm{cov}} = 0$ is fully back-to-back; $\tau_{\mathrm{cov}} = 1$
  is (at least) fully matched. In Phase B this is the hedge ratio $\alpha$;
  in Phase C it is derived from inventory $k$ and demand $N \cdot P$.
- **Barrier $h$, level $H = h \cdot S_0$.** §3e switching strategy. The
  intermediary flips from fixed-quote principal to live-cost fee the first
  time $S_t$ crosses the upper barrier $H$. $h$ is the dimensionless ratio
  over the spot at $t = 0$; $h = \infty$ disables the switch.
- **Stopping time $\tau := \inf\{t : S_t \ge H\} \wedge T$.** First-passage
  time of $S_t$ to the §3e barrier, truncated at the horizon. Distinct from
  the §3c coverage fraction $\tau_{\mathrm{cov}}$: here $\tau$ carries units
  of time (0 ≤ $\tau$ ≤ $T$) and indexes a random event, not a deterministic
  schedule.
- **$I_\tau := \int_0^\tau S_t\,dt$, $J_\tau := \int_\tau^T S_t\,dt$.**
  Pre- and post-switch slices of the price integral; $I_\tau + J_\tau = I_T$
  exactly. In §3c with deterministic coverage we recycle the same letter
  pair replacing $\tau$ by $\tau_{\mathrm{cov}} \cdot T$.
- **VaR$_{95}$ (Value-at-Risk).** The loss threshold exceeded with 5%
  probability — the 5th percentile of P&L, sign-flipped. Says
  *where* the bad tail begins, not how deep it goes.
- **CVaR$_{95}$ (Conditional VaR / Expected Shortfall).** Mean loss
  *inside* that worst 5%. Strictly more informative than VaR once the
  tail is fat or skewed. VaR$_{99}$ / CVaR$_{99}$ are the same at the
  1% level.
- **Fee revenue, not P&L.** $R_{\mathrm{fee}} = f \cdot P \cdot \lambda
  \cdot I_T$ is a revenue, so $R_{\mathrm{fee}} \geq 0$ by construction.
  Its VaR and CVaR are therefore the *low end of revenue* — "worst
  case is still a gain, just a smaller one" — not a loss against zero.
